title: Watch Bison Fend Off a Wolf Attack on a Newborn Calf
gist: A 2025 trail-camera video from a Polish forest, published in Ecology and Evolution, captured a wolf pack attacking a newborn European bison calf — surprising because bison, thanks to their size, are rarely considered wolf prey; a 2014 study found it takes 9-13 wolves to bring one down versus two for an elk.

themes.theme: Bison as an unlikely wolf-predation target

insights.claim: A trail camera in a Polish forest captured video, shot in 2025 and published in the journal Ecology and Evolution, of a wolf pack attacking a newborn European bison calf — a notable event because European bison have not typically been considered prey animals for wolves.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Documents a shift or gap in understanding of predator-prey dynamics for a species long assumed near-invulnerable due to size, relevant to wildlife-conservation and ecological-risk modeling in European forests.
insights.quote: European bison haven’t typically been considered prey animals
insights.locator: A bison calf narrowly avoided a pack of ravenous wolves

insights.claim: A 2014 study of wolves hunting North American bison found it takes a pack of roughly nine to thirteen wolves to successfully bring down a bison, compared to as few as two wolves for an elk.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Quantifies the predation-cost asymmetry between prey species, explaining why bison herding behavior and sheer group size function as an effective defense against all but the largest wolf packs.
insights.quote: it takes a pack of around nine to 13 wolves for a successful bison hunt
insights.locator: Per a 2014 study of wolves hunting North American bison

insights.claim: Bison, like other herding animals, congregate in large groups to reduce individual predation risk, but this same strategy has co-evolved with larger wolf packs becoming more successful at bringing down the herd's members.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Illustrates a co-evolutionary arms-race dynamic between herd defense and pack-hunting scale — a reusable framework for thinking about safety-in-numbers strategies that shift the threshold rather than eliminate the risk.
insights.quote: the “strength in numbers” axiom cuts both ways
insights.locator: Like other herding animals, bison congregate in large groups

top_entities: Mammal Research Institute, Polish Academy of Sciences
